schools.summary: Chrysostom: Pentecost the harvest feast, the Spirit the keen sickle of the word; Cyril of Jerusalem: the ascended Lord fulfils the promise of the Comforter; Leo: a chief feast.
'Fully come' (en to synplerousthai); the feast drew the largest crowds of distant pilgrims — the audience for the wonder.
Wesley: the Pentecost of Sinai (legal, terrible) and of Jerusalem (evangelical, merciful) set side by side.
'Suddenly' (aphno) a roar 'as of a rushing mighty wind,' recalling Sinai (1 Kgs 19:11) and the Spirit at creation; Cyril of Jerusalem: a heavenly trumpet signifying the Spirit's presence.
Calvin: the gift made visible/audible as a condescension to human dullness, that God's power not pass away unknown.

Better 'tongues as of fire, parted' (not 'cloven') — distributing, one resting on each; tongues the emblem of the languages, fire the Spirit's purifying, kindling power; each disciple received the gift personally.

They spoke real, intelligible languages not their own, as the Spirit gave utterance; Babel reversed — grace gathers the nations by giving all speech; the miracle in the speakers' mouths (Wesley), the outward sign of a greater inward fullness.
Devout Jews of the dispersion and proselytes from every nation, settled or come for the feast, each fluent in a land's tongue — the providential polyglot audience for the gift of tongues.
Calvin: God in all ages gathered to Jerusalem some preserved seed of his people.
The 'voice' (phone, an utterance, not rumour) drew the multitude, confounded because each heard his own language — undeniable and public.
Calvin: the gathering into one place made the miracle the more famous and beyond dispute.

They 'stood out of themselves' (existanto) in amazement that Galilaeans — proverbial for rude dialect and no learning (Mark 14:70) — spoke the world's languages; the contrast proving the gift divine, a sign to unbelievers (1 Cor 14:22).
Each heard 'his own tongue wherein he was born'; debate whether the wonder lay in speaking or hearing (the better view in the apostles' speaking) — the gospel meeting every man in his mother tongue.

The catalogue (Parthians, Medes, Elamites, Mesopotamia, Judaea, Asia Minor) traces the dispersion from the east westward — Luke counting the languages to show the gift's reach; even Judaea's differing dialect included.

The list widens to Africa (Egypt, Cyrenaic Libya) and to sojourning Romans; 'Jews and proselytes' marks that Gentile converts were present too — a quiet foreshadowing of the wider ingathering and the gospel's march to Rome.
Cretes and Arabians close the list; 'the wonderful works of God' = his majesty/great deeds — words of praise, not yet teaching (which comes through Peter's sermon).
Calvin: the double wonder — lowly Galileans treating of deep divine things, and in foreign tongues.
'Were perplexed' (dieporounto, wholly at a loss); 'what meaneth this?' = 'what does this wish to be?' — honest bewilderment that owns the wonder and seeks its meaning.
Calvin: God's majesty could scarcely appear more plainly than in this miracle — to remain unmoved would be monstrous.
'Mocking' (diachleuazontes) — the charge of 'new wine' (gleukous, sweet but intoxicating); Wesley traces the world's descent from mockery to slaughter.
Cyril of Jerusalem: they spoke truer than they knew — the disciples were filled with new wine, the grace of the New Covenant from the true Vine.

themes: the new wine of the Spirit

schools.summary: Peter rises with the eleven as spokesman — the timid denier now bold, with elaborate discourses (Ellicott); a formal, impressive beginning, with the utmost sobriety (Wesley).
Chrysostom: divinely ordered that some mocked, to give Peter a starting-point for his defence.
A quick common-sense reply: only the third hour (9 a.m.), before any Jew would be drunk, especially on a feast; drunkenness belonged to the night (1 Thess 5:7) — the slander refutes itself.
Cyril of Jerusalem: the hour significant — crucified at the third hour, he now sends grace at the third hour.

'This is that' — a positive interpretation by the Messianic prophecy of Joel 2:28, Peter's mind opened by the Spirit; the wonder a fulfilment, the hinge from sign to Scripture and the method of apostolic preaching.

Joel's 'afterward' = 'the last days,' the days of the Messiah now begun; the Spirit poured on 'all flesh' — every age, sex, and rank; sons and daughters prophesy, the gift once confined to a few now spread on all.
Calvin: the prophecy belongs to the last age alone — the era of the outpoured Spirit.
The culmination — the Spirit poured even on bondservants, male and female, by a God no respecter of persons; the Jews had thought prophecy beyond the poor, a barrier now swept away.
Calvin: the promise restrained to God's worshippers, those made his servants by the Spirit — yet within that company none excluded by station.
'Wonders' (terata) and 'signs' (semeia); many refer the portents to the calamities before Jerusalem's fall (Matt 24:7); great grace attended with great judgment on those who reject it (Wesley).
Calvin: the 'great day' comprehends the whole kingdom of Christ, not one coming alone.

The sun's darkening and moon's bloody hue (an eclipse's phenomena, Matt 24:29) — emblems of calamity heralding the day of the Lord; Peter binds these portents to the Messianic age Pentecost inaugurates.
'Whosoever shall call on the name of the Lord shall be saved' — universal and free (cf Rom 10:12); to 'call upon' implies the whole of religion, faith in prayer (Wesley).
Calvin: after the terrors, God shows salvation near and attainable — only call upon him.
Jesus 'approved/accredited of God' (apodedeigmenon) by miracles done openly in their midst — God's own testimony, known to all; the boldness of naming him seven weeks after the cross (Ellicott).
Chrysostom: 'men of Israel' not flattery but tact, easing toward them before naming Jesus.

Delivered up (ekdoton) by 'the determinate counsel and foreknowledge of God,' yet crucified by 'wicked hands' — the cross both God's ordained plan and man's guilty act; 'foreknowledge' Peter's word (1 Pet 1:2).
Calvin: the death named to make the resurrection the more assuredly believed.
God raised him, loosing 'the pains/pangs of death,' for death could not hold him; the first public, testable proclamation of the resurrection (Robertson), made openly in Jerusalem.
Calvin: 'the sorrows of death' more than bodily — the horror of God's curse, which Christ bore and overcame.

Peter reads Ps 16:8-11 as David's, written of the Messiah — David's confidence of preservation a type of Christ; the OT itself foretelling the resurrection, quoted carefully that hearers may weigh it.

Heart, tongue (Hebrew 'glory'), and flesh rejoice and rest 'in hope' — not a mortal's dread but confident expectation of rising; the body reposing in hope of resurrection, fulfilled in Christ not David.

'Hell' = Hades, the unseen world of the dead (not punishment); God will not leave the Holy One's soul there nor let his body see corruption — fulfilled exactly in Christ; his soul in paradise (Luke 23:43).

'The ways of life' = resurrection (the way back from death); 'joy with thy countenance' = the gladness of the exalted Christ at God's right hand — words fitting the risen Lord, not David in the grave.
Calvin: restored from death to life by the grace of God.

Peter speaks 'freely/boldly' (meta parresias): David is dead and buried, his tomb still among them — the psalm's 'no corruption' untrue of David (whose body decayed), so it must speak of another; addressing them as 'brethren.'

David 'being a prophet' (2 Sam 23:2) knew God's sworn oath (Ps 132:11) that a descendant would sit on his throne — fulfilled in the eternal King, the Christ; the clause naming Christ doubtful in the best MSS but the argument stands.

David, 'foreseeing' (proidon) as a prophet, spoke of the resurrection of the Christ — soul not left in Hades, flesh not corrupted (cf 1 Pet 1:11); the prophets testified beforehand of Christ's sufferings and glory.

'This Jesus hath God raised up, whereof we all are witnesses' — prophecy joined to eyewitness fact; the apostles' primary office is to be witnesses of the resurrection, the united public testimony of the whole company.

Exalted 'by/to the right hand of God,' the place of supreme power, the risen Jesus received the promised Spirit (John 14:16) and shed him forth — Pentecost the visible proof of Christ's exaltation and reign.
Calvin: a wonderful work of God, exalting his Christ — thought destroyed by death — to so great glory.

David did not ascend, yet calls another 'my Lord' (Ps 110:1, the most-cited Messianic text), the words by which Jesus confounded the scribes (Matt 22:44) — the psalm of the enthroned Lord points beyond David to Christ.
Cyril of Jerusalem: the Lord says it to the Lord — the Father to the Son, not to a servant.

'Thy foes thy footstool' — the conqueror's image of total subjection; the reign lasts till the kingdom's purpose is complete (1 Cor 15:25); a warning that the hearers' continued opposition courts certain ruin.
The climax: God has made 'both Lord and Christ' the very Jesus 'whom ye crucified' — the deliberate sting in the word-order; all the proofs converge, pressing the hearers to a verdict.
Calvin: the one they spitefully handled they must now acknowledge as their Lord.
'Pricked' (katenygesan, pierced, stung sharply) in heart by cool, pungent reasoning under the Spirit; they cry 'what shall we do?' now calling the apostles 'brethren' — conviction, not despair only.
Chrysostom: gentleness, more than vehemence, pricks the heart and inflicts the keener wound.

'Repent' (turn mind and life, now) and be baptized in Jesus' name for remission of sins, and receive the gift of the Spirit — the same indispensable repentance as John's baptism; the 'gift' the saving Spirit, not merely tongues (Wesley).

The promise to 'you,' 'your children,' and 'all afar off' — the horizon widening to the Gentiles; all may claim it as fully as the apostles, against every sectarian narrowing; joined to 'as many as the Lord shall call.'
Calvin: added that the Jews might be sure the grace of Christ belonged to them as to the apostles.

themes: to your children and afar off

schools.summary: Luke gives only an outline ('many other words'); Peter 'continued to testify and exhort'; 'save yourselves' is passive 'be ye saved' — submit to God's salvation and flee the judgment on a perverse generation; gospel preaching as pressing entreaty.
They 'gladly' (freely, cheerfully, without compulsion) received the word and were baptized — about 3,000 added that day; Lightfoot hears the 'willing people in the day of thy power' (Ps 110:3).
Calvin: one sermon gained 3,000, who with prompt and ready mind embraced his word — the mark of true faith.

They 'continued steadfastly' (proskarterountes) in four marks — apostles' doctrine, fellowship (koinonia), breaking of bread, and prayers; perseverance, not passing enthusiasm; the abiding pattern of Christian community.
Henry: the truly primitive church kept close to holy ordinances and abounded in pious devotion.

'Fear' (reverential awe, not terror) came on every soul, silencing mockery; the apostles' signs and wonders 'kept on coming' (imperfect) — the more wonders, the more fear; the miracles authenticating the word.

'Were together' (one in faith and purpose) and 'had all things common' — not enforced communism nor abolished property, but goods held loosely for the common good; a fulfilment of Luke 12:33, a society of sympathy and self-denial.

'Sold' and 'parted' (imperfect — an ongoing practice, not one act) lands and goods, distributing 'as every man had need'; no law compelled it, love constrained them (Wesley); measured by need, a wise and generous love.

Daily 'with one accord in the temple' (no breach yet with Judaism) and 'breaking bread from house to house' (the regular meal), eating with 'gladness and singleness of heart' — devotion and joy blended in everyday life.
Calvin: they frequented the temple for the opportunity to further the gospel, not from the holiness of the place.

Praising God and 'having favour with all the people' (their devoted lives winsome, cf Luke 2:52); the Lord 'kept on adding' (imperfect) daily 'such as should be saved' — genuine conversions, the church's increase God's own work.
Calvin: the fruit of an innocent life is to find favour even among strangers.
